perturbation.original_text: The Master Franchise Agreement requires application of the laws of New York. This provision may not be enforceable under California law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Master Franchise Agreement suggests application of the laws of New York. The enforceability of this provision under California law may be subject to interpretation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text acknowledges potential unenforceability under California law. The modified text uses 'suggests' and 'subject to interpretation,' creating ambiguity about which laws actually govern, potentially misleading the franchisee about their rights under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5 addresses choice of law provisions in franchise agreements. The modification creates uncertainty and ambiguity about the applicable law, potentially discouraging a franchisee from asserting their rights under California law as the enforceability of the New York choice-of-law provision is left open to 'interpretation'. This may conflict with California's interest in protecting franchisees within its borders.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 20040.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 8. SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 8. Venue of Disputes [20040.5- 20040.5.] ( Article 8 added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1277, Sec. 1. ) 20040.5. A provision in a franchise agreement restricting venue to a forum outside this state is void with respect to any claim arising under or relating to a franchise agreement involving a franchise business operating within this state. (Added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1277, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 1995.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the applicable law, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5, which aims to protect franchisees in California. The original text acknowledges potential unenforceability, while the modified text uses softer language ('suggests', 'subject to interpretation'), potentially discouraging a franchisee from asserting their rights under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'A provision in a franchise agreement restricting venue to a forum outside this state is void with respect to any claim arising under or relating to a franchise agreement involving a franchise business operating within this state.' The ambiguity created in the changed text could allow the franchise agreement to be interpreted in a way that circumvents California law, contradicting the protective intent of BPC 20040.5.

perturbation.original_text: You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. California Corporation Code 31512 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516). Business and Professions Code 20010 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043).
perturbation.changed_text: You might be asked to sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. The implications of California Corporation Code 31512 and Business and Professions Code 20010 on such a release are complex and require further evaluation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that California law voids waivers of rights. The modification weakens this statement by using 'might be asked' and stating that the implications are 'complex and require further evaluation,' creating ambiguity about the franchisee's rights and suggesting that waivers might be enforceable, despite California law to the contrary.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code Section 31512 explicitly voids any condition, stipulation or provisions binding any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of the California Franchise Investment Law. The altered text, implying a need for 'further evaluation,' introduces ambiguity regarding the franchisee's protections against waiving these rights, which conflicts directly with the legal protection ensured by the California Corporations Code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 31512 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 4. SECURITIES [25000 - 31528] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 1949, Ch. 384. ) DIVISION 5. FRANCHISE INVESTMENT LAW [31000 - 31528] ( Division 5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) PART 6. GENERAL PROVISIONS [31510 - 31516] ( Part 6 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) 31512. Any condition, stipulation or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of this law or any rule or order hereunder is void. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the franchisee's protection against waiving rights, directly contradicting California Corporations Code Section 31512. The original text clearly states that waivers are void, while the changed text suggests 'further evaluation' is needed, implying that waivers might be enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'Any condition, stipulation or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of this law or any rule or order hereunder is void.' This clear and direct conflict between the changed text and the law creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Master Franchise Agreement contains a liquidated damages clause. Under California Civil Code Section 1671, certain liquidated damages clauses are unenforceable.
perturbation.changed_text: The Master Franchise Agreement may contain a liquidated damages clause. The enforceability of any such clause under California Civil Code Section 1671 is subject to judicial interpretation and may depend on specific circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'contains' to 'may contain', the text introduces uncertainty about the existence of a liquidated damages clause. Further, replacing 'are unenforceable' with 'is subject to judicial interpretation and may depend on specific circumstances' creates ambiguity about the franchisee's rights and the enforceability of such clauses, despite California Civil Code Section 1671 setting clear guidelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Civil Code Section 1671 does allow for enforceable liquidated damages clauses in certain situations (specifically, when determining actual damages would be impractical or extremely difficult), the altered text broadly suggests that judicial interpretation is always necessary and that the enforceability always 'depends on specific circumstances.' This is misleading because, in many common franchise scenarios, such clauses are deemed unenforceable unless meeting stringent requirements, and the alteration downplays this legal protection. The ambiguity could deter a franchisee from challenging an unlawful liquidated damages clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1671 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4.5. LIQUIDATED DAMAGES [1671 - 1681] ( Heading of Title 4.5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1671- 1671.] ( Heading of Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) 1671. (a) This section does not apply in any case where another statute expressly applicable to the contract prescribes the rules or standard for determining the validity of a provision in the contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract. (b) Except as provided in subdivision (c), a provision in a contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract is valid unless the party seeking to invalidate the provision establishes that the provision was unreasonable under the circumstances existing at the time the contract was made. (c) The validity of a liquidated damages provision shall be determined under subdivision (d) and not under subdivision (b) where the liquidated damages are sought to be recovered from either: (1) A party to a contract for the retail purchase, or rental, by such party of personal property or services, primarily for the party’s personal, family, or househ
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the existence and enforceability of liquidated damages clauses, weakening the legal protection provided by California Civil Code Section 1671. The original text clearly states that the Master Franchise Agreement 'contains' such a clause and that under Section 1671, such clauses 'are unenforceable'. The modification introduces uncertainty by stating the agreement 'may contain' such a clause and that its enforceability 'is subject to judicial interpretation and may depend on specific circumstances.' This contradicts the original text which implies a more direct enforceability standard. The law snippet 1 from CIV 1671(b) states that a liquidated damages provision is valid unless proven unreasonable, but subdivision (c) points to specific scenarios (retail purchase, personal property rental) where the validity is determined under (d) and not (b). The altered text creates ambiguity that doesn't align with the specific guidelines of section 1671.
